title: From Defeat to Occupation
subtitle: MacArthur as viceroy, Article 9, land reform, the Korean War boom that ignited the recovery
bullets: On August 15, 1945, Emperor Hirohito broadcast the surrender speech in formal courtly Japanese — most Japanese had never heard his voice before, and most could not fully understand what he was saying.

MacArthur and SCAP governed Japan as a single American-led occupation for nearly six years, the only postwar Axis country not partitioned among multiple Allied powers, with MacArthur ruling personally from the Dai-Ichi Building opposite the Imperial Palace.

MacArthur deliberately chose not to prosecute Hirohito as a war criminal, against substantial Allied opposition; the Tokyo War Crimes Trials of 1946–48 hanged seven senior leaders including former Prime Minister Tojo Hideki but left the throne intact as a constitutional figurehead.

Article 9 of the 1947 Constitution renounces war as a sovereign right and prohibits the maintenance of land, sea, and air forces — the only such clause in any major national constitution, and despite seventy-five years of pressure it has never been amended.

SCAP land reform forced absentee landlords to sell tenant-farmed land to the government at fixed prices for resale to the tenants on long-term credit, turning roughly 90% of Japanese tenant farmers into owner-occupiers within two years and breaking the rural landlord class.

Joseph Dodge's 1949 austerity program crushed Japan's hyperinflation through a balanced budget and the fixed 360-yen-to-dollar exchange rate that would undervalue the yen for the next twenty-two years and quietly underwrite the entire export-led growth era.

The Korean War procurement boom flooded Japan with roughly $2.3 billion in special procurement orders between 1950 and 1953, doubling industrial production and rebuilding the steel and engineering sectors at American expense.

Yoshida Shigeru reportedly called the Korean War 'a gift from the gods' — for Korea the war was a national catastrophe, for Japan eight years after defeat it was an economic deliverance, and the asymmetry shaped Japanese-Korean relations for decades.

title: The Yoshida Doctrine and the Korean War Boom
subtitle: Minimum rearmament, the American security umbrella, and the LDP-dominated 1955 System that would govern Japan for thirty-eight years
bullets: Yoshida Shigeru, twice prime minister and the central architect of the postwar settlement, had been imprisoned briefly by the wartime military police in April 1945 — the wartime imprisonment turned out to be the best thing that ever happened to his postwar career.

The Yoshida Doctrine had three components — minimum rearmament, the American alliance as security umbrella, and total economic focus — and guided Japanese foreign policy from 1951 through the end of the Cold War, conserving fiscal resources by holding defense spending to roughly 1% of GDP.

The 1954 Self-Defense Forces were established under the legal fiction that purely defensive forces did not violate Article 9, with strict prohibitions on overseas deployment and offensive weapons systems — a fiction that has been steadily relaxed but never officially abandoned.

The November 1955 merger of the Liberal Party and Democratic Party into the Liberal Democratic Party created the dominant party that has governed Japan continuously for thirty-eight years, organized around competitive factions rather than between parties.

Japan's postwar democracy operated as a 'one-and-a-half party system' — a dominant LDP plus a permanently uncompetitive Japan Socialist Party — that produced extraordinary policy stability while frustrating Western political scientists looking for democratic alternation.

The 1960 Anpo Protests against the U.S.-Japan Security Treaty renewal mobilized 30 million Japanese in petitions and hundreds of thousands at the Diet — Tokyo University student Kanba Michiko was crushed to death on June 15, and President Eisenhower's planned visit was cancelled.

Kishi Nobusuke — Tojo's wartime commerce minister, three-year Sugamo Prison war-crimes suspect, and the most aggressive LDP factional leader pushing for constitutional revision — rammed the treaty through the Diet by force and resigned three weeks later, his career destroyed by the way he had won.

The Anpo crisis settled the basic shape of Japanese politics for thirty years: the LDP would govern, the security alliance would continue, but constitutional revision and substantial rearmament were politically impossible — a verdict the conservative LDP has not been able to reverse since.

title: Income Doubling and the High-Growth Years
subtitle: Ikeda's Income Doubling Plan, MITI's industrial guidance, the Toyota Production System, and the 1964 Tokyo Olympics that announced postwar Japan to the world
bullets: Ikeda Hayato launched the Income Doubling Plan in December 1960, six months after taking office in the Anpo aftermath, committing the government to doubling per-capita real income within ten years — Japan actually doubled it in seven.

MITI directed Japanese industrial policy through low-interest Japan Development Bank loans, foreign-currency licensing, technology import approvals, import controls, and informal administrative guidance — building the steel, shipbuilding, auto, and electronics sectors that defined the high-growth era.

The keiretsu system of horizontally federated industrial groups — Mitsubishi, Mitsui, Sumitomo, Fuyo, Sanwa, Daiichi-Kangyo — replaced the prewar zaibatsu, with main-bank lending and cross-shareholdings making hostile takeovers nearly impossible and tying Japanese banks to long-term industrial commitment.

The salaryman system combined lifetime employment, seniority pay, enterprise unions, and total identification of personal interest with corporate interest — supported on the household side by the sengyo shufu professional housewife managing finances and child-rearing.

The Toyota Production System developed by engineer Ohno Taiichi combined just-in-time inventory, jidoka stop-the-line authority for any worker, and kaizen continuous improvement — letting Toyota produce vehicles with one-third the labor hours of American manufacturers and becoming the global manufacturing standard by 2000.

Sony's TR-55 transistor radio in 1955 established the formula that would define Japanese consumer electronics for forty years: license a foreign technology, miniaturize and improve it, manufacture at scale with high quality, undercut on price, win the global market.

The 1964 Tokyo Olympics opened on October 10 with nineteen-year-old Sakai Yoshinori — born in Hiroshima on the day of the atomic bombing — lighting the Olympic flame as the symbolic emblem of postwar rebirth.

The Tokaido Shinkansen opened on October 1, 1964 as the world's first high-speed rail, covering 320 miles between Tokyo and Osaka at speeds up to 130 mph and carrying over 10 billion passengers across the next sixty years without a single fatal accident from train collision or derailment.

By 1968 Japan had passed West Germany to become the world's second-largest economy by GDP, behind only the United States — closer to West Germany than to Britain in per-capita income and closing fast on both.

title: Japan as Number One
subtitle: The 1973 oil shock survived, the export juggernaut, trade friction with America, and the Plaza Accord that set up the bubble
bullets: The 1973 oil shock hit Japan harder than any other major industrial economy — the country imported 99% of its oil, with roughly 80% from the Middle East — and crude prices quadrupled within months, ending the high-growth era and triggering the 1974 GDP contraction.

Japan adapted faster than any Western competitor through aggressive energy efficiency investment that halved energy use per unit of GDP between 1973 and 1990, plus structural transition from heavy industry to high-value-added cars and electronics.

The Honda Civic, released in July 1972 just before the oil shock, established Japanese automakers as serious U.S. competitors — by 1980 Japanese makers held over 20% of the U.S. auto market and Detroit was in structural crisis.

Voluntary export restraints capping Japanese auto exports to the U.S. at 1.68 million vehicles per year pushed Japanese makers upmarket into Acura, Lexus, and Infiniti and into building U.S. plants — Honda Marysville (1982), Nissan Smyrna (1983), Toyota Georgetown (1988) — that produced 1.5 million American-built Japanese vehicles annually by the late 1980s.
The Sony Walkman released in July 1979 sold over 200 million units across all variants, transformed how people listened to music, and was the direct ancestor of the iPod twenty-two years later.

Ezra Vogel's 1979 book Japan as Number One sold over a million copies in Japan and crystallized a brief American intellectual moment of treating Japanese institutions as a model rather than a competitor — the moment closed by the early 1990s.

The 1986 U.S.-Japan Semiconductor Trade Agreement was the first time the United States demanded — and won — guaranteed market share inside Japan for American chips, after Japanese firms had captured over 50% of the global memory market.

Vincent Chin, a Chinese American draftsman in Detroit, was beaten to death in June 1982 by two unemployed white autoworkers who reportedly mistook him for Japanese — his killers received probation and a $3,000 fine, galvanizing Asian American civil rights organizing.

The September 1985 Plaza Accord pushed the yen from 240 to 121 per dollar within twenty-eight months, the Bank of Japan responded with aggressive interest rate cuts to support exporters, and the resulting cheap-credit boom inflated the asset bubble that would burst in 1990–91.

title: The Bubble Years
subtitle: The Nikkei peaks at 38,957, Mitsubishi buys Rockefeller Center, Murakami Haruki, and the Showa-Heisei transition that bracketed the era
bullets: The Nikkei 225 rose from 13,113 in January 1986 to a peak of 38,957 on December 29, 1989 — a 197% rise that made Tokyo's stock market briefly larger than New York's despite a Japanese economy half the size of America's.

At peak valuation in late 1989, the grounds of the Imperial Palace were notionally worth more than all real estate in California, the Ginza commercial district's land was valued at over $200,000 per square foot, and the cumulative value of all Japanese land was theoretically four times the total land value of the United States despite Japan being twenty-six times smaller.

Mitsubishi Estate's $846 million purchase of Rockefeller Center in October 1989 was the most symbolic of a wave of Japanese trophy purchases — Sony's Columbia Pictures, Matsushita's MCA, Pebble Beach golf course, and Vincent van Gogh's Portrait of Dr. Gachet for $82.5 million — most of which lost money spectacularly.

Murakami Haruki's 1987 novel Norwegian Wood sold over 4 million copies in Japan within a few years, made him the dominant literary figure of the bubble generation, and launched the international career that would make him the most globally read Japanese author of any era.

Otomo Katsuhiro's 1988 animated feature Akira was the first Japanese animated feature to find a substantial Western adult audience and is conventionally cited as the start of the global anime wave that has since reached cultural saturation in markets that know nothing else about Japan.

Novelist Oe Kenzaburo won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1994 — the second Japanese laureate after Kawabata Yasunari in 1968 — bracketing the high-growth and bubble eras with the two literary works most engaged with what postwar Japan had become.

The 1988–89 Recruit Scandal implicated Prime Minister Takeshita Noboru, Finance Minister Miyazawa, and former Prime Minister Nakasone in receiving pre-IPO shares at insider prices, accelerating the public disillusionment with LDP politics that would briefly cost the party power in 1993.

Emperor Hirohito died on January 7, 1989 after a sixty-three-year reign — the longest in Japanese imperial history — that had spanned militarism, total war, surrender, occupation, the economic miracle, and the bubble peak.

The Heisei era opened on January 8, 1989 with Akihito's accession but in retrospect became the era of the Lost Decades — the bubble burst within thirty months of its start, and the entire era was characterized by stagnant GDP, deflation, and demographic decline.

title: Daily Life and Culture
subtitle: The salaryman day, examination hell, the train system, kawaii, baseball, and Japanese cuisine going global
bullets: The bubble-era salaryman left home at 6:30 a.m., commuted into central Tokyo on packed rush trains where station attendants pushed passengers onto carriages, worked until 8 or 9 p.m., drank with colleagues until 11, and caught the last train home around 1 a.m. — rarely seeing his children awake on weekdays.

Karoshi — death from overwork, typically by stroke or heart attack triggered by chronic sleep deprivation — became a recognized medical and legal category in the 1980s as the human cost of the salaryman system became visible.

The 1985 Equal Employment Opportunity Law was the first formal step toward integrating women into the salaryman career track, but in practice firms used the two-track system to channel women into clerical jobs with no promotion ladder and an implicit expectation they would resign upon marriage.

The juken jigoku examination-hell system funneled Japanese children from elementary school through cram-school juku into a tiered exam-defined university hierarchy — admission to Tokyo, Kyoto, Hitotsubashi, Keio, or Waseda effectively determined lifetime career options.

Tokyo's Shinjuku station handled roughly 3.5 million passengers daily by the bubble's end — the busiest train station in the world — with average system punctuality measured in seconds rather than minutes and Yamanote Line waits of approximately 90 seconds regardless of time of day.

Kawaii cute culture, commercialized at industrial scale by Sanrio with Hello Kitty in 1974, became a defining Japanese aesthetic export by the 1990s — applied to consumer products, mascots, fashion, and eventually the Cool Japan national-branding policy of the 2000s.

By the mid-1980s Japan had achieved the world's highest vending machine density at roughly one machine per 23 people, selling everything from drinks and cigarettes to hot meals, umbrellas, and adult magazines — the combination of low street crime, high real-estate costs, and salaryman lifestyles made the vending economy uniquely Japanese.

Japanese cuisine globalized through the 1970s and 1980s — Kikkoman built soy sauce factories in Wisconsin in 1973, the California roll popularized sushi to American palates in the 1980s, and washoku was inscribed on UNESCO's intangible cultural heritage list in 2013.

Soka Gakkai, a Nichiren Buddhist lay organization claiming over 8 million households by 1980, founded the Komeito political party in 1964 — Komeito has been a continuous coalition partner with the LDP since 1999 and is the closest thing to a religious-political mass movement in postwar Japanese politics.

Novelist Mishima Yukio's seppuku at the Self-Defense Forces' Ichigaya headquarters on November 25, 1970 — after a failed coup attempt to restore imperial powers and abolish Article 9 — was the most spectacular Japanese suicide since General Nogi's in 1912 and the conservative-literary mirror of the radical-left Zengakuren collapse the year before.

title: The Burst
subtitle: The 1990 crash, the bad-loan overhang, the Yamaichi collapse, the Kobe earthquake, and the Aum Shinrikyo subway sarin attack that defined the era of unease
bullets: The Bank of Japan raised the discount rate from 2.5% to 6.0% in five steps between May 1989 and August 1990, the Ministry of Finance imposed quantitative limits on real-estate lending in March 1990, and both policies designed to deflate the bubble gradually worked far faster than expected.

The Nikkei 225 lost 38% in 1990 alone — the largest one-year decline in any major equity market since the 1929 American crash — and as of 2026 has only just clawed back to its 1989 peak, meaning a generation of Japanese investors has earned essentially zero nominal equity returns over thirty-six years.

The Lost Decade was characterized by stagnant 1% growth, persistent mild deflation, ballooning bank bad debt, and serial fiscal stimulus packages that pushed Japanese government debt past 100% of GDP by 2000 — the Lost Decade eventually became Lost Decades as the pattern continued through the 2000s and 2010s.

Yamaichi Securities collapsed in November 1997 after concealing roughly 260 billion yen in losses through tobashi schemes — president Nozawa Shohei's tearful press-conference apology became one of the defining images of the financial crisis.

The Great Hanshin earthquake on January 17, 1995 killed over 6,400 people in the Kobe-Osaka region, exposed structural weaknesses in Japan's emergency response, and produced the iconic visual image of a section of the elevated Hanshin Expressway toppled onto its side.

The Aum Shinrikyo subway sarin attack on March 20, 1995 killed thirteen people and injured over 6,000 — the doomsday cult under Asahara Shoko had recruited extensively among science-and-engineering graduate students at elite Japanese universities, whose chemistry expertise built the cult's chemical-weapons capability.

Asahara Shoko and twelve associates were captured in May 1995 and finally executed in July 2018, ending a twenty-three-year legal process that had become one of the most extensively documented modern terrorism cases.

In the July 1993 lower-house election the LDP lost its majority for the first time since 1955, ending the 1955 System; coalition governments held office until 1996 when the LDP returned to power, but in a permanently weakened structural position dependent on coalition partners.

JSP leader Murayama Tomiichi became prime minister of an LDP-JSP-Sakigake coalition in 1994 — the Socialists abandoned acceptance of the Self-Defense Forces, the U.S.-Japan alliance, the Hinomaru flag, and the Kimigayo anthem as the price of holding office, ending the postwar political polarity that had defined the 1955 System.

title: Legacy
subtitle: What the miracle accomplished, the demographic crisis already visible in 1991, and what comes next for Japan and for everywhere else
bullets: The postwar Japanese miracle was the most successful national reconstruction in modern history — a defeated, occupied, atomic-bombed country turned itself in roughly thirty years into the world's second-largest economy with infrastructure and consumer products demonstrably ahead of most Western competitors.

Article 9 has constrained Japanese military policy across seven decades, prevented Japan from participating in major American military operations in any combat capacity, and despite repeated LDP attempts has never been amended — the supermajority of public opinion across seventy years has consistently opposed amendment by margins of two or three to one.

The Yoshida Doctrine's bargain — minimum rearmament, American security umbrella, full economic focus — paid off enormously in the high-growth era; whether it will continue to pay off in a regional environment of Chinese expansion and an unpredictable American partner is a question Japanese strategists are actively debating.

The institutional template that postwar Japan invented — state-coordinated industrialization, undervalued currency, export orientation, mass education, family-controlled industrial groups — became the standard manual for late industrialization that South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, and post-1978 China would all study and adapt.

Japan's total fertility rate dropped to 1.57 in 1989 — the 1.57 shock that produced the first widespread public awareness of the aging-society problem — and the demographic projections from 1991 turned out to be correct: population peaked in 2008 at 128 million and has been falling since.

Japan has now been the world's first developed country to live with effective zero growth, near-zero interest rates, a shrinking population, and structural deflation for over thirty years — the textbook predictions of debt crisis, hyperinflation, and institutional collapse have not materialized.

The Japan that emerged from Heisei was, by most quality-of-life metrics, a genuinely good place to live — extremely low crime, excellent public services, advanced healthcare, internationally competitive infrastructure, vibrant cultural production — and the Heisei stagnation was a stagnation around an extraordinarily high baseline.

The era ended because the institutional design that had produced it had reached its operating limits — the keiretsu, the salaryman, MITI, the LDP, the lifetime-employment system that had each been a high-growth-era asset had each by 1991 also become a high-growth-era constraint.

Almost every common statement about the Japanese economic miracle is at least partly a myth: the cultural-essentialist explanation is wrong (the methods were transferable, and the same productivity is now achieved in Tennessee, Munich, and Wuhan), the bubble was not obvious in retrospect, and the Lost Decade was not a uniquely Japanese failure — almost every developed country has now followed Japan into the same demographic and macroeconomic environment.
